title: Isotopes
summary: Cambridge 9701 AS — isotopes, relative atomic mass, and why isotopes share chemical properties but differ in mass. Includes PhET Isotopes and Atomic Mass simulation.
learningObjectives: Define isotopes as atoms with the same number of protons but different numbers of neutrons.
Calculate relative atomic mass from isotopic abundances.
Explain why isotopes have identical chemical properties.
State uses of radioactive isotopes in medicine and industry.

simpleExplanation.title: Same element, different mass
simpleExplanation.summary: Isotopes are atoms of the same element with different neutron counts. They behave identically in chemical reactions because chemistry is about electrons, not neutrons.
steps: Keep proton number fixed — only change neutrons to make isotopes of one element.
Relative atomic mass is the weighted average of isotope masses by natural abundance.
Chemical properties match because electron configuration (and Z) are the same.
Some isotopes are unstable and decay — used in PET scans, radiotherapy, and dating.

flashcards.back: Atoms of the same element (same Z) with different numbers of neutrons (different A).
Same electron configuration — chemical behaviour depends on electrons.

sections.content: **Isotopes** appear in almost every 9701 Paper 1 multiple-choice set and in structured Ar calculations on Paper 2. The key distinction: **same proton number, different neutron number**.
Ar = Σ (isotope mass × fractional abundance). Abundances must sum to 100% (or 1.0 as fractions).

sections.question: Bromine has two isotopes: 79Br (50.7%) and 81Br (49.3%). Calculate Ar.

sections.content: When Ar is quoted to one decimal place in the Periodic Table (e.g. Cl = 35.5), it reflects isotope mixtures — not rounding error. Show your weighted-mean working for M marks.
